summary: Stancil argues that the Big Data era is ending not because data got less important, but because the industry finally shifted from grand, hype-driven promises to boring, practical tools like Snowflake and dbt. The real breakthrough is lowering friction and cost so “ordinary” teams can do more with familiar workflows, which will quietly unlock new use cases over time. He contends Databricks and similar tools should stop selling visionary narratives and instead sell themselves as simple, better databases.
money_quotes: Databricks is a $38 billion dollar mistake.
But we never connected these dots during our evaluation because the story Databricks told was buzzwords.
Snowflake’s was boring, and all we wanted—at least at first—was boring.
By following the same path, Big Data is finally starting to live up to its potential.
With Snowflake—and hopefully, with Databricks—we don’t get flying cars or predicting the future; we just get a little less headache.
key_insight: Big Data’s promise is being realized not through revolutionary new paradigms, but through boring, reliable infrastructure that makes familiar analytics cheaper and easier.
